d:info.d:title: (U) Data Element Dictionary for Data Element Dictionary XML

d:entry.d:para: (U) A wrapper element for showing the classification of an Element as a whole and for grouping the values relating to an element.
(U) A container holding a set of resource-level information relevant to the entire Data Element Dictionary. This captures the following information concepts:
(U) The person or organization that Authored the DED.

(U) The prose that identifies and describes the reason and scope of the model (e.g., the output) about which this Data Element Dictionary is providing guidance. If the source of the information contained in the DED is different from the DedHeader/edh/ResponsibleEntity, then the author should identify said source.
(U) The short abbreviation that captures the intent of the DedTitle of the Data Element Dictionary (This value MUST be unclassified).

(U) A short descriptor that characterizes the format and/or structure of the output about which this Data Element Dictionary is providing guidance. The initial possible values are: Logical, UML, Physical, Ontology, Database, XML, JSON, CSV, CVE, and Other:SomeText. Multiple values may be used. Additional details about the DED Model should be added to DedPurpose.
(U) The descriptive phrase that indicates the content of the Data Element Dictionary.
(U) The short string that represents the unique state of the Data Element Dictionary.
(U) Additional clarifying notes, beyond what is contained within the Definition. Also, may contain implementation guidance or processing instructions.
(U) A grouping of additional clarifying notes, beyond what is contained within the Definition. Also, may contain implementation guidance or processing instructions.
(U) The format of the allowed values for the Data Element. (e.g., string, integer, float, uuid, datetime, boolean, alphanumeric, enumeration, regex).
(U) The value that should be provided for the Data Element, if the Data Element does not exist or have content.
(U) A statement of the meaning of the Data Element, explaining what it is and/or its essential characteristics.
(U) The cited authority(ies) or reference(s) for which the knowledge to develop the ElementDefinition was obtained or derived.
(U) The context within which the Data Element belongs.
(U) The locally unique identifier of the Data Element.

(U) This element follows the UML section 7.8.8 (MultiplicityElement) definition "A multiplicity is a definition of an inclusive interval of non-negative integers beginning with a lower bound and ending with a (possibly infinite) upper bound. A MultiplicityElement embeds this information to specify the allowable cardinalities for an instantiation of the Element." The exact tailoring of the UML is shown in the DES. Example notations [0..1], [0..*], [0..n] [1..1], [1..*], [1..n], [n..m]. Note that n and m here are variables that represent finite numbers. Actual use in a DED would replace n and m with the finite number(s) appropriate for the Element. For example, [0..6] means the Element is optional and can have up to 6 instances. [1..5] means the Element is required and can have up to 5 instances.
(U) The string identifier that represents the Data Element.
(U) The kind of Data Element that is being defined (e.g., class, attribute, element, keyword, table).

(U) An indicator of whether the Data Element MUST be populated in all cases (i.e., Mandatory); or if the Data Element is NOT required (i.e., Optional); or whether specific conditions for populating it are specified in ElementRestriction (i.e., Conditional). If conditional, then the condition should be defined in either ElementRestrictions or the Relationship Metadata section.
(U) The ElementName(s) of another Data Element within the Data Element Dictionary, for which this Data Element is a child element.

(U) The additional constraints, if any, on the content entered for the Data Element (e.g., the maximum number of characters, the regex, uniqueness, min and/or max values, precision, scale). Special Cases: 1) If the DataType is an enumeration, then provide either (A) the enumerated list, or (B) a reference to a specific enumerated list; 2) If the DataType is a regex, then provide the actual regular expression.

(U) A grouping of the additional constraints, if any, on the content entered for the Data Element (e.g., the maximum number of characters, the regex, uniqueness, min and/or max values, precision, scale). Special Cases: 1) If the DataType is an enumeration, then provide either (A) the enumerated list, or (B) a reference to a specific enumerated list; 2) If the DataType is a regex, then provide the actual regular expression.
(U) The kind of determinate quantity or magnitude, taken as a standard of comparison for others of the same kind, in assigning to them numerical values, such as inches, hours, miles.
(U) A wrapper element for showing the classification of a Relationship as a whole and for grouping the values relating to a relationship.
(U) Additional clarifying notes, beyond what is contained within the RelationshipDefinition or the Relationship Multiplicities. Also, may contain implementation guidance or processing instructions.

(U) A grouping of additional clarifying notes, beyond what is contained within the RelationshipDefinition or the Relationship Multiplicities. Also, may contain implementation guidance or processing instructions.
(U) The additional information of how Link A and Link B interact with each other, beyond multiplicity information.
(U) The cited authority(ies) or reference(s) for which the knowledge to develop the RelationshipDefinition was obtained or derived.
(U) The locally unique identifier of the Relationship.
(U) The context within which the Data Element A belongs.

(U) This element follows the UML section 7.8.8 (MultiplicityElement) definition "A multiplicity is a definition of an inclusive interval of non-negative integers beginning with a lower bound and ending with a (possibly infinite) upper bound. A MultiplicityElement embeds this information to specify the allowable cardinalities for an instantiation of the Element." The exact tailoring of the UML is shown in the DES. Example notations [0..1], [0..*], [0..n] [1..1], [1..*], [1..n], [n..m]. Note that n and m here are variables that represent finite numbers. Actual use in a DED would replace n and m with the finite number(s) appropriate for the Relationship. For example, [0..6] means the Relationship is optional and can have up to 6 instances. [1..5] means the Relationship is required and can have up to 5 instances. This element indicates the multiplicity of instances of A that must exist for a single instance of B and the greatest (or maximum) number of instances of A that can exist for a single instance of B.
(U) The string identifier that represents the Data Element A.
(U) The context within which the Data Element B belongs.

(U) This element follows the UML section 7.8.8 (MultiplicityElement) definition "A multiplicity is a definition of an inclusive interval of non-negative integers beginning with a lower bound and ending with a (possibly infinite) upper bound. A MultiplicityElement embeds this information to specify the allowable cardinalities for an instantiation of the Element." The exact tailoring of the UML is shown in the DES. Example notations [0..1], [0..*], [0..n] [1..1], [1..*], [1..n], [n..m]. Note that n and m here are variables that represent finite numbers. Actual use in a DED would replace n and m with the finite number(s) appropriate for the Relationship. For example, [0..6] means the Relationship is optional and can have up to 6 instances. [1..5] means the Relationship is required and can have up to 5 instances. This element indicates the multiplicity of instances of B that must exist for a single instance of A and the greatest (or maximum) number of instances of B that can exist for a single instance of A.
(U) The string identifier that represents the Data Element B.
(U) The string identifier that represents the Relationship.
